message.content:
You are an adversarial fact-checker continuing a research verification pass from a saved checkpoint.

1. Read the file C:\Users\Dan\Desktop\WebDev\IslandLife\research\checkpoints\all-claims.json (a JSON array of {source, claim, quote}). Keep ONLY entries whose source is exactly: https://selfdeterminationtheory.org/SDT/documents/2010_PrzybylskiRigbyRyan_ROGP.pdf
2. Fetch that URL with WebFetch. If it is paywalled, JS-only, a video page (e.g. GDC Vault), or otherwise inaccessible, set accessible=false and adjudicate each claim ONLY on internal consistency between the claim and its saved quote: quote clearly supports claim => SUPPORTED with note 'quote-consistent, source inaccessible'; quote contradicts or does not contain the load-bearing element => UNVERIFIABLE. Never invent page content.
3. If accessible, actively try to REFUTE each claim against the page text. Mark SUPPORTED only if every load-bearing element (numbers, sample sizes, named attributions, causal direction) is backed by the text. Mark REFUTED only when the text contradicts the claim. Mark UNVERIFIABLE when the text neither supports nor contradicts.
4. Return one verdict per claim via StructuredOutput. Repeat each claim's first ~150 characters verbatim so it can be matched back.
PRIOR VERIFICATION CONTEXT (you may confirm quickly rather than re-litigate, but still sanity-check each claim): Two claims survived 3-0 adversarial voting in run 1 (SDT need-satisfaction model; three needs independently predict enjoyment/immersion/future play).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- document-skills:algorithmic-art: Creating algorithmic art using p5.js with seeded randomness and interactive parameter exploration. Use this when users request creating art using code, generative art, algorithmic art, flow fields, or particle systems. Create original algorithmic art rather than copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- document-skills:brand-guidelines: Applies Anthropic's official brand colors and typography to any sort of artifact that may benefit from having Anthropic's look-and-feel. Use it when brand colors or style guidelines, visual formatting, or company design standards apply.
- document-skills:canvas-design: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. You should use this skill when the user asks to create a poster, piece of art, design, or other static piece. Create original visual designs, never copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- document-skills:claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements). TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project. SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- document-skills:doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- document-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- document-skills:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- document-skills:internal-comms: A set of resources to help me write all kinds of internal communications, using the formats that my company likes to use. Claude should use this skill whenever asked to write some sort of internal communications (status reports, leadership updates, 3P updates, company newsletters, FAQs, incident reports, project updates, etc.).
- document-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- document-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- document-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- document-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- document-skills:slack-gif-creator: Knowledge and utilities for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack. Provides constraints, validation tools, and animation concepts. Use when users request animated GIFs for Slack like "make me a GIF of X doing Y for Slack."
- document-skills:theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- document-skills:web-artifacts-builder: Suite of tools for creating elaborate, multi-component claude.ai HTML artifacts using modern frontend web technologies (React, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui). Use for complex artifacts requiring state management, routing, or shadcn/ui components - not for simple single-file HTML/JSX artifacts.
- document-skills:webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs.
- document-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-1050 of 1499 total (30311 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=1051 limit=1050 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	A Motivational Model of Video Game Engagement
2	Andrew K. Przybylski
3	University of Rochester
4	C. Scott Rigby
5	Immersyve, Inc., Celebration, Florida
6	Richard M. Ryan
7	University of Rochester
8	More Americans now play video games than go to the movies (NPD Group, 2009). The meteoric rise in
9	popularity of video games highlights the need for research approaches that can deepen our scientiﬁc
10	understanding of video game engagement. This article advances a theory-based motivational model for
11	examining and evaluating the ways by which video game engagement shapes psychological processes
12	and inﬂuences well-being. Rooted in self-determination theory (Deci & Ryan, 2000; Ryan & Deci,
13	2000a), our approach suggests that both the appeal and well-being effects of video games are based in
14	their potential to satisfy basic psychological needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness. We
15	review recent empirical evidence applying this perspective to a number of topics including need
16	satisfaction in games and short-term well-being, the motivational appeal of violent game content,
17	motivational sources of postplay aggression, the antecedents and consequences of disordered patterns of
18	game engagement, and the determinants and effects of immersion. Implications of this model for the
19	future study of game motivation and the use of video games in interventions are discussed.
20	Keywords: self-determination theory, motivation, video games
21	Psychological research examining video games is presently un-
22	dergoing a dramatic shift in focus. Until very recently, the pre-
23	ponderance of research in video games has been concern-focused,
24	with studies aimed at identifying the potential negative effects of
25	gaming. Speciﬁc foci of these research programs have included the
26	relations between gaming and increased aggression, social isola-
27	tion, and overuse (Anderson & Bushman, 2001; Gru ¨sser, Thale-
28	mann, & Grifﬁths, 2007). Yet, more recently, a number of re-
29	searchers have become intervention-focused, hoping to harness the
30	magnetic motivational appeal of video games to help relieve pain
31	and stress or customizing games for educational or health-related
32	interventions (for a review, see Baranowski, Buday, Thompson, &
33	Baranowski, 2008). Increasingly, intervention-focused researchers
34	are demonstrating that games can positively inﬂuence both psy-
35	chological and physical well-being.
36	Although the goals of both concern-focused and intervention-
37	focused research are dissimilar, both share a descriptive research
38	approach: The methods and theories they employ evaluate the
39	extent to which video games exert positive, negative, or no inﬂu-
40	ence on speciﬁed outcomes under a given set of circumstances.
41	What is less well understood and less widely studied are the
42	mechanisms that underlie these positive and negative links.
43	In this article, we outline a theory-based empirical model for
44	understanding and evaluating the processes through which video
45	games motivate sustained engagement, and how experiences with
46	these games affect the psychological and physical well-being of
47	players. Our approach is based on self-determination theory (SDT;
48	Deci & Ryan, 2000; Ryan & Deci, 2000a), a macrotheory of
49	human motivation that is principally concerned with the potential
50	of social contexts to provide experiences that satisfy universal
51	human needs. We apply that theory to an understanding of how
52	video gaming contexts satisfy or thwart psychological needs and
53	thus foster or undermine sustained engagement and either positive
54	or negative well-being outcomes for players. We present both
55	direct and indirect empirical evidence that highlights the utility of
56	our motivational approach, and show that it can inform such
57	controversial topics as the appeal of violent games, the linkages
58	between aggression and violent games, and the causes and conse-
59	quences of disordered patterns of game engagement. The empiri-
60	cally based model we present here generates a number of novel
61	and testable hypotheses, and thus has the potential of informing
62	game-based interventions and supplying a better understanding of
63	the positive and negative inﬂuences that video games exert on
64	psychological and physical well-being.
65	Motivation and Video Games
66	Video games motivate a remarkable amount of goal-directed
67	behavior. For example, every week well over 10 million players of
68	the popular online game World of Warcraft forgo other opportu-
69	nities for leisure and invest more than 225 million hours collabo-
70	rating, exploring, and competing against one another in a virtual
71	world. Why do so many people spend so much time engaged in
72	such games? The immediate and most obvious answer is “because
73	they are fun.” Indeed, video game play differs markedly from
74	activities that are engaged in for some form of external reward
75	(e.g., payment for work). Few video game players receive any
76	Andrew K. Przybylski, and Richard M. Ryan, Department of Clinical
77	and Social Sciences, University of Rochester; C. Scott Rigby, Immersyve,
78	Inc., Celebration, FL.
79	Correspondence concerning this article should be addressed to Andrew
80	K. Przbylski, Clinical and Social Sciences in Psychology, University of
81	Rochester, 355 Meliora Hall, Rochester, NY 14627. E-mail: andrew
82	.przybylski@gmail.com
83	Review of General Psychology © 2010 American Psychological Association
84	2010, Vol. 14, No. 2, 154 –166 1089-2680/10/$12.00 DOI: 10.1037/a0019440
85	154
86	
87	external rewards for play; indeed, many pay tangible (e.g., sub-
88	scription fees, game costs) and social (e.g., angry parents, lost
89	social time) costs to play. Instead, the appeal of video games lies
90	in the inherent properties of the experiences they provide.
91	Although video games are a relatively new entertainment me-
92	dium, the empirical study of behaviors pursued because “they are
93	fun” is not new. According to SDT (Ryan & Deci, 2000b), behav-
94	iors pursued for their own sake or their inherent satisfaction are
95	identiﬁed as intrinsically motivated , whereas those pursued to
96	access desired end states or avoid aversive ones are understood as
97	extrinsically motivated. For more than 3 decades, one subtheory of
98	SDT, cognitive evaluation theory (CET), has guided research on
99	intrinsic motivation in sports, education, and leisure domains. For
100	example, Ryan and Deci (2007) recently reviewed a substantial
101	body of research on sport and exercise in which CET explained
102	interest and sustained participation in these activities. In another
103	recent example, Sheldon and Filak (2008) experimentally manip-
104	ulated autonomy, competence, and relatedness support in a non-
105	digital game-based learning context, showing that each of these
106	basic needs independently predicted motivation for a game activ-
107	ity. This extensive body of research based on CET thus shows that
108	speciﬁc psychological nourishments present in activities are nec-
109	essary for activities to be experienced as inherently enjoyable or
110	fun, and it is these nourishments that inﬂuence the effects the
111	activities have on motivation and well-being.
112	CET-based research demonstrates that activities foster greater
113	intrinsic motivation to the extent to which they satisfy three
114	fundamental human needs: the need for competence (sense of
115	efﬁcacy), autonomy (volition and personal agency), and related-
116	ness (social connectedness; Ryan & Deci, 2000a). When activities
117	make rewards, punishments, and self-esteem pressures salient,
118	they foster controlled forms of extrinsic motivation. Contrary to
119	what may be assumed, these two forms of motivation are not
120	additive. In fact, factors that can enhance extrinsic motivation,
121	such as rewards, pressures, or evaluations, typically undermine
122	intrinsic motivation (Deci, Koestner, & Ryan, 1999). Research has
123	also shown that these forms of motivation have very different
124	inﬂuences on individuals engaging in the same activity. Compared
125	with those pursuing an activity for extrinsic reasons, intrinsically
126	motivated actors enjoy the activity more, are more creative, dem-
127	onstrate more cognitive ﬂexibility, process information more care-
128	fully and completely, and incur greater psychological and physical
129	health beneﬁts (Ryan & Deci, 2000a).
130	The motivational model we propose for video games is based on
131	the premise that the principles of CET and SDT that have been
132	applied to the study of intrinsically motivated behaviors are also
133	relevant to the psychological functioning and well-being of players
134	engaged with video games. In support of this stance, we begin with
135	a review of the brief history of video games, and how their
136	development has increasingly enabled them to satisfy the basic
137	psychological needs speciﬁed within SDT. We then turn to recent
138	empirical work based on SDT that evaluates the need-satisfying
139	potential of video games and the relations of need satisfaction with
140	players’ intrinsic motivation and well-being. We suggest that the
141	SDT model of need satisfaction predicts sustained engagement
142	over time, as well as the short-term effects of game activities on
143	players’ well-being.
144	Expanding on this application of our approach, we extend our
145	need-based model to some concern-focused questions currently
146	being examined in the ﬁeld. For instance, we examine violent
147	game content and its contribution to motivation for and enjoyment
148	of video games. Next, we present research that evaluates sources
149	of player aggression as a consequence of video game engagement.
150	Speciﬁcally, our interest is in the relations between game experi-
151	ences that are psychologically need-thwarting and postgame ag-
152	gressive feelings. We then shift focus and explore how player
153	backgrounds shape game engagement, contrasting players who are
154	playing because they want to and those who feel compelled or
155	pressured to play or are playing because they must . Next, we
156	review research exploring how a sense of immersion in virtual
157	contexts shapes the inﬂuence of virtual content on real-world
158	outcomes. Finally, we close with a discussion of how our model
159	can help advance future basic research on video games, and we
160	highlight the relevance of our model for both concern-focused and
161	intervention-focused research questions.
162	Psychological Need Satisfaction in Video Gaming
163	Contexts: A Historical Look
164	There is good reason to expect that video games have the
165	potential to satisfy basic psychological needs. Indeed, the ever-
166	broadening appeal of games suggests that video games tap into
167	motivational processes as well as or in some cases better than
168	traditional forms of media entertainment. In fact, a brief historical
169	review suggests that since their inception video games have in-
170	creasingly tapped into the basic psychological needs speciﬁed
171	within SDT. Speciﬁcally, the industry started with games tailored
172	to meet competence needs through games focused on challenges
173	and goals to be mastered. Over time, video game developers have
174	broadened game designs and environments to better meet the
175	autonomy need by providing ﬂexibility in goals, choice over
176	strategies, and opportunities for action in novel environments.
177	Along with expanding autonomy, games have also increasingly
178	been apt at satisfying relatedness needs by providing opportunities
179	for engaging in online interactions and communities. We discuss
180	each of these phases and the needs they meet in turn.
181	Competence Need
182	The earliest successful video games excelled at supporting the
183	fundamental human need for competence. The developers of coin-
184	operated games such as Galaga, Pong, and Donkey Kong that
185	populated arcades in the 1970s and early 1980s structured these
186	virtual environments so that reﬂex-based challenges gradually
187	increased in line with the player’s progress through the game. The
188	pacing of challenges was designed so players could continually
189	experience enhanced competence as they progressed in the game,
190	with challenges increasing apace with player ability. This balanc-
191	ing of game difﬁculty and player skill was critical to the success of
192	arcade games; if the challenges underwhelmed players, they would
193	lead to boredom, and if they overwhelmed the player, they would
194	generate frustration. Developers sought to avoid either in order to
195	hold the interest and loyalty of players.
196	Although the contemporary game industry has moved away
197	from arcade settings, balancing player skill against game chal-
198	lenges remains a prime concern. For example, modern console-
199	based games, such as HALO 3 , use Internet technology to match
200	155SPECIAL ISSUE: A MOTIVATIONAL MODEL OF VIDEO GAME ENGAGEMENT
201	
202	players against one another on the basis of their history of in-game
203	performance. In addition to deriving new ways to balance chal-
204	lenges with ability, games have become more sophisticated in how
205	they provide performance feedback and acknowledge the prowess
206	of players. Some games approach feedback in general terms,
207	whereas others provide granular and timely feedback in terms of
208	points or indicators built into the game world. Microsoft’s online
209	gaming network provides a general indicator of players’ skill by
210	aggregating each player’s performance across all of the Xbox 360
211	games a user owns and calculates an overall score. The music
212	game Guitar Hero uses the in-game audience of nonplaying char-
213	acters to provide continuous feedback as these characters cheer
214	and scream if the player performs well (or boos and hisses if the
215	player falters). Because skill-graded challenges and positive feed-
216	back are key to player experiences of competence need satisfaction
217	(Ryan & Deci, 2000a), they represent an important element in
218	motivating play.
219	Autonomy Need
220	Whereas early arcade games offered players a relatively con-
221	strained set of choices, as video game play moved from an arcade
222	to a home-based activity in the 1980s and ’90s, it also began to tap
223	into the basic psychological need for autonomy. Games such as
224	Final Fantasy, The Legend of Zelda , and Adventure placed players
225	in the role of ﬁctional characters and provided a wide range of
226	in-game choices over goals and strategies and varied opportunities
227	for action. These games drew on a wide talent base that included
228	programmers, writers, and artists. Together, these teams created
229	expansive virtual contexts, unexplored worlds populated by char-
230	acters, and fantasy narratives. Creators of these games fostered a
231	sense of equiﬁnality (multiple routes to an end) for players in terms
232	of overall achievement and supported autonomy giving players
233	options over multiple game elements: what missions they choose,
234	the skills they acquire, and how their characters appear. By en-
235	abling players to advance through different game challenges, al-
236	lowing them to form and break alliances with nonplayer charac-
237	ters, players were empowered to shape the game’s narrative. The
238	creators of modern games aim to provide players with meaningful
239	choices to continuously balance their boundless curiosity against a
240	ﬁnite pool of resources and talent. For example, one recently
241	created game, Spore, adopts a novel approach to this dilemma.
242	Instead of relying on game professionals to place all of the objects
243	and creatures in the Spore’s world, the game uses a technology
244	called procedural generation, whereby novel environments are
245	dynamically generated by the software using content created by
246	other players of the game around the world. In this way, the game
247	strives to generate almost limitless new content, and thus supports
248	opportunity and choice for each player.
249	Relatedness Need
250	Social interaction has always been an important part of video
251	game play, both in arcade settings and home console games. Only
252	recently, however, have popular games provided players opportu-
253	nities to interact and connect within large shared virtual worlds
254	while at the same time occupying geographically remote real-
255	world locations. Early computer terminal-based games such as
256	Zork and MUD1 were hosted by university computer mainframes
257	and provided dozens of players with text-based (i.e., nongraphical)
258	game worlds populated with both computer- and human-controlled
259	characters. Modern incarnations of multiplayer games, such as
260	World of Warcraft, give players connected to the Internet access to
261	a central virtual world that provides opportunities for both com-
262	petitive and cooperative group play. These games allow players to
263	band together in short-term groups to accomplish missions in a
264	single gaming session and form longer term groups, known as
265	guilds, factions, or clans, that persist over time. Game developers
266	have increasingly brought Internet-based technologies to support
267	longer term relationships between players, including Web forums,
268	guild chat channels, and voiceover Internet protocol communica-
269	tion. These features enable players to develop social bonds, allow-
270	ing them to cooperate in person or with tens of thousands of
271	geographically remote peers.
272	Mastery of Controls
273	One key characteristic that distinguishes real-world from gam-
274	ing contexts is that the latter lack physical substance. This differ-
275	ence carries two practical implications for the study of human
276	engagement and motivation in games. First, player actions are
277	mediated through a control interface, and second, player actions
278	cannot readily capitalize on the sense of proprioception as a source
279	of control and feedback. As such, players do not have a fully
280	intuitive sense of orientation and action in virtual environments,
281	and they must invest time and energy to master the control inter-
282	face and learn the mechanics of each game. In the course of
283	studying video game motivation, we termed the learned ability to
284	effortlessly perform intended actions in the game’s virtual envi-
285	ronment as mastery of controls .
286	Mastery of controls plays an important role in game motivation,
287	largely as a necessary, but not sufﬁcient, condition for achieving
288	psychologically need-satisfying play. Video game players and
289	designers refer to the process of acquiring mastery of controls
290	using the term learning curve . Games that have complex controls
291	and mechanics are said to have a “steep” learning curve, and
292	players generally do not enjoy this kind of engagement, instead
293	viewing it as a price of admission to what is hoped will be future
294	fulﬁlling game experiences. Once obtained, mastery of controls
295	provides video game players with the chance to access developer
296	affordances such as exploration, puzzle solving, or emerging vic-
297	torious in combat. Therefore, although control mastery is not
298	implicitly satisfying, it unlocks the game’s potential to meet the
299	player’s psychological needs.
300	In recent years, game interfaces have become increasingly so-
301	phisticated and intuitive. Initially, control interfaces like those
302	provided by arcade games were simple, but each game required
303	players to learn and use different constellations of joysticks and
304	buttons. Later, platforms standardized interfaces across games to
305	facilitate player interface competence, allowing them to transfer
306	skills from one game to another. Although some early games like
307	Duck Hunt used inherently intuitive interfaces (the NES Zapper ),
308	such games were relatively rare until recently. Modern interfaces
309	such as Nintendo’s motion-tracking Wii controller and those pat-
310	terned after musical instruments for games such as Rock Band and
311	Guitar Hero reduce the burden of learning how to play, thereby
312	allowing novice and experienced players to focus on playing well.
313	156 PRZYBYLSKI, RIGBY, AND RYAN
