315	Research on Need Satisfaction in Games
316	According to our motivational model, video games have the
317	potential to enhance intrinsic motivation and short-term well-being
318	insofar as they provide players with experiences that satisfy uni-
319	versal psychological needs. This perspective emphasizes motiva-
320	tional processes inherent to the structure of gaming contexts. To
321	say this another way, it is our view that games are generally more
322	or less appealing, and have a greater or lesser inﬂuence on player
323	well-being, as a function of the extent to which the in-game
324	experiences they provide fulﬁll fundamental psychological needs.
325	The ﬁrst published research applying this model consisted of
326	four studies drawing on between- and within-persons research
327	designs, varied samples, and experimental methods to gain a broad
328	idea of the motivational principles at play in video games (Ryan,
329	Rigby, & Przybylski, 2006). As part of this work, we designed and
330	validated measures of need satisfaction within video game con-
331	texts that we called the player experience of need satisfaction .I n
332	addition to measuring autonomy, competence, and relatedness
333	needs, these assess issues related to mastery of controls and play-
334	ers’ experience of immersion. These assessments are used in the
335	studies we review in this article unless otherwise indicated.
336	We formulated and tested three main hypotheses in this initial
337	research. First, we expected that need-satisfying experiences
338	within video game play would contribute to (a) intrinsic motiva-
339	tion for play, (b) immersion in gaming environments, and (c)
340	positive short-term shifts in player well-being. Second, we ex-
341	pected that the motivational affordances provided by games un-
342	derlie their appeal over and above the content of games and
343	individual differences. Finally, we expected that mastery over
344	game controls and mechanics was necessary to access need-
345	satisfying opportunities provided by games, but that an intuitive
346	grasp of game interface was not a sufﬁcient condition for enhanc-
347	ing player motivation or well-being.
348	In the ﬁrst study, we assigned novice video game players to 40
349	min of video game engagement within a 3-D “platform” game that
350	emphasized exploration and skill-based challenges. We expected
351	that self-reports of autonomy and competence need satisfaction
352	would relate to positive short-term shifts (pre- to postplay) in
353	well-being, greater game enjoyment, and a greater likelihood that
354	participants would elect to return to the game when given a choice
355	to do so. Results conﬁrmed these predictions and showed that the
356	experience of autonomy need satisfaction during play related pos-
357	itively to change in affect and enjoyment of the game. In addition
358	to these outcomes, experiences of competence need satisfaction
359	related to greater immersion in the game world, increased self-
360	esteem, and the likelihood of reengaging the game in a free-choice
361	period.
362	A second study in the Ryan et al. (2006) series focused on how
363	between-games differences in psychological need satisfaction re-
364	lated to their popular appeal. We created a within-subjects design
365	that randomly assigned participants to play one of two games on
366	their ﬁrst visit to our lab and the other on their second visit. We
367	selected two games that were closely matched on content but
368	dissimilar in terms of their critical acclaim— one being a commer-
369	cial and critical hit and the other a ﬂop. We expected that the
370	variability that existed between these games in terms of popularity
371	reﬂected underlying differences in the extent to which each was
372	psychologically need satisfying. Results supported this intuition.
373	Analyses conducted on the between-games level demonstrated that
374	participants experienced greater levels of autonomy and compe-
375	tence need satisfaction playing the popular game. Analyses con-
376	ducted on the within-person (between-games) level showed that
377	differences in game enjoyment, immersion, as well as pre- to
378	postplay shifts in well-being were directly associated with vari-
379	ability in the need satisfaction each game provided. In other words,
380	the more popular game had a more positive inﬂuence on short-term
381	well-being and was more intrinsically motivating precisely be-
382	cause it provided experiences that were richer in autonomy and
383	competence need satisfaction.
384	In a third study, we focused on isolating the unique contribu-
385	tions made by need-satisfying play from variability based on
386	differences in game content, player skill, or player demographics.
387	Thus, our third study statistically modeled the relations between
388	in-game need satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and well-being
389	outcomes using multilevel modeling, analyzing these links in
390	terms of person-nested and game-nested effects (Bryk & Rauden-
391	bush, 1992). Participants came to our lab on four separate occa-
392	sions and completed measures of well-being; then they were
393	randomly assigned to play a different game on each visit before
394	completing postplay assessments like those from the ﬁrst two
395	studies. We expected that experiences of autonomy and compe-
396	tence need satisfaction during play would relate to greater game
397	enjoyment, immersion, preference for future play, and short-term
398	increases in well-being. Between-persons analyses showed that
399	players who experienced greater levels of autonomy and compe-
400	tence need satisfaction experienced greater game enjoyment, pref-
401	erence for future play, and immersion independent of variance
402	yoked to speciﬁc game contexts. Of greater interest were within-
403	person, between-games analyses that showed that play experiences
404	that supported the autonomy and competence needs produced
405	more enjoyable, more immersive play that was enhancing for
406	player vitality, self-esteem, and affect. We found that psycholog-
407	ically satisfying experiences of play were a robust predictor of
408	motivation and well-being across individuals and across the varied
409	game contents and narratives.
410	A ﬁnal study by Ryan et al. (2006) extended the model to
411	self-selecting video game players to see whether motivation and
412	well-being were similarly linked in this population. In addition,
413	because the prior studies had focused only on autonomy and
414	competence needs, we wanted to examine how opportunities for
415	players to satisfy the need for relatedness were also associated with
416	motivation and well-being. Results demonstrated that all three
417	needs made independent contributions to game enjoyment, immer-
418	sion, and estimations of future engagement. Furthermore, although
419	relatedness need satisfaction was correlated with positive well-
420	being outcomes, only autonomy and competence need satisfaction
421	derived from play accounted for unique incremental variance in
422	player affect following play.
423	In three of these studies, we speciﬁcally evaluated the motiva-
424	tional role ﬁlled by mastery of controls, deﬁned as the sense one
425	has intuitive command over a game’s basic mechanics and control
426	interface. Our main prediction was that mastery of controls con-
427	stituted a necessary but not sufﬁcient condition for video game
428	engagement to satisfy psychological needs, be enjoyable, and bare
429	positively on well-being. Results supported this, showing mastery
430	of controls related to enjoyment and other outcomes as expected,
431	157SPECIAL ISSUE: A MOTIVATIONAL MODEL OF VIDEO GAME ENGAGEMENT
432	
433	but it no longer accounted for unique variance in player motivation
434	and well-being when in-game need satisfaction was considered.
435	Together, these studies showed that psychologically need-
436	satisfying experiences form the root of intrinsically motivating
437	play, and that such experiences positively inﬂuence short-term
438	shifts in well-being and increased immersion in the game world.
439	The studies suggest that the gaming environments, like their real-
440	world counterparts, can be understood in terms of psychological
441	needs, and that these frustrations and affordances provide a robust
442	account for player motivation and the effects of play on wellness
443	outcomes.
444	The Motivational Appeal of Violence in Games
445	Perhaps the most controversial aspect of video games is that so
446	many involve conﬂict, combat, crime, and war. Violent games
447	such as these have been widely studied, but this inquiry largely has
448	focused on the effects violent games may have on player aggres-
449	sion outside of games (Anderson et al., 2004; Ferguson, 2007;
450	Sherry, 2001). Some experimental studies have suggested that
451	violent game play can be a cause of short-term shifts in aggression
452	(Anderson et al., 2004), and others have shown no such effect
453	(Ferguson, 2007). Longitudinal research ﬁndings are similarly
454	mixed; some researchers have found that video game exposure
455	contributes to subsequent player aggression (Anderson, Gentile, &
456	Buckley, 2007), although others have found that violent game play
457	does not relate to greater aggression over long spans of time in
458	studies that randomly assigned individuals to play different games
459	(D. Williams & Skoric, 2005). If indeed violent game play and
460	aggression are related, the causal direction of such links is unclear.
461	Because our model is focused on motivation, we became inter-
462	ested initially in a slightly different question. In particular, we
463	wondered whether violence played a motivational role in account-
464	ing for the appeal of games; that is, does violent content lead to
465	more interest or sustained engagement in video games? Many
466	authors have suggested that the violent imagery and narratives
467	present in games are motivating in their own right (Kirsh, 1998;
468	Olson, 2007; Zillman, 1998). Yet, according to our SDT view,
469	violence itself does not fulﬁll basic psychological needs and there-
470	fore should not motivate play by itself. At the same time, our
471	review of video games suggests that many features afforded by
472	popular violent games could hold the potential to satisfy psycho-
473	logical needs. For example, games involving conﬂict and combat
474	can readily support the need for autonomy by empowering the
475	player with opportunities for action, choices over strategies and
476	missions, and relatively open environments in which to act. Sim-
477	ilarly, in-game carnage often serves to enhance the need for
478	competence by providing players with intuitive and immediate
479	feedback about their performance. Seeing blood, for example,
480	immediately tells a player that he or she has hit the target. Simi-
481	larly, team-based challenges can foster camaraderie and bolster
482	relatedness in struggles against a common foe. Thus, our interest
483	was in disentangling the relationship of need satisfaction to vio-
484	lence, to see whether violence per se provided any motivational
485	appeal.
486	Given these questions, in our next series of studies (Przybylski,
487	Ryan, & Rigby, 2009), we formulated and tested three primary
488	hypotheses concerning the motivational appeal of violence in
489	games. First, we expected that the core appeal of violent video
490	games would be based on the experiences of need satisfaction they
491	provided; in other words, we anticipated game preferences and
492	enjoyment would be largely a function of basic need satisfaction.
493	Second, we hypothesized that the level of violence in games would
494	relate to intrinsic motivation and immersion in gaming environ-
495	ments only insofar as it was reducible to need satisfaction. Third,
496	we predicted that individuals high in dispositional aggression
497	would opt into and value violent games to a greater extent because
498	violent content ﬁt with their trait-like styles, whereas people low in
499	trait aggression would not prefer such games. To this end, we
500	created six studies that evaluated the inﬂuence of violent game
501	content on motivation and immersion during play.
502	In a ﬁrst study, we evaluated the motivational appeal of violent
503	game content and experiences of need satisfaction across a broad
504	spectrum of games. The central question was whether the level of
505	violence present in a game is a consistent motivator? We surveyed
506	a large number of players about their current favorite game and
507	collected self-reports of intrinsic motivation, immersion, and like-
508	lihood of recommending the game. Level of violence in games was
509	quantiﬁed using two coding schemes. First, we applied ratings
510	provided by the Entertainment Software Ratings Board for each
511	game, which assesses violent and sexual content. We also applied
512	our own rating system that focused on the intensity and graphic-
513	ness of the violence itself. We predicted that level of violence
514	could be correlated with motivation, but would not be uniquely
515	linked when basic need satisfaction was accounted for. Results
516	largely supported this expectation, showing that player experiences
517	that supported the autonomy and competence needs related to
518	greater intrinsic motivation, environmental immersion, and word-
519	of-mouth recommendations for games. Furthermore, results
520	showed that level of violence was only weakly linked to immer-
521	sion and was entirely unrelated to intrinsic motivation and word-
522	of-mouth recommendations. These ﬁndings did not allow us to
523	evaluate our hypothesis that the appeal of violence was reducible
524	to need satisfaction because there was no relation to deconstruct or
525	mediate. In addition, the null relation between level of game
526	violence and motivation ran counter to the prevailing view that the
527	presence of violence in games adds to their appeal.
528	Our subsequent studies focused on further zeroing in on the
529	common belief that more game violence creates a more motivating
530	play experience. In our second study, the central question we asked
531	was whether game violence motivates some people but not others.
532	We thus broadened the scope of our inquiry by assessing trait-level
533	aggression. Trait-level aggression represents a stable individual
534	difference that reﬂects variability in perceiving and reacting to the
535	world in aggressive or hostile ways (Buss & Perry, 1992). We thus
536	viewed trait aggression as a likely moderating factor that could
537	account for a link between level of violence in games and some
538	individuals’ preference for them. Speciﬁcally, we hypothesized
539	that play experiences that provided satisfaction of the needs for
540	competence and autonomy would consistently predict intrinsic
541	motivation, environmental immersion, and preference for future
542	play, but we had no expectation that these should relate directly to
543	violence. That is, we expected to ﬁnd that, on average, violent
544	games are no more preferred or need satisfying than nonviolent
545	ones. Yet, we also reasoned that trait-level aggression would
546	moderate preference for future play such that highly aggressive
547	individuals would prefer games that embed challenges and choices
548	158 PRZYBYLSKI, RIGBY, AND RYAN
549	
550	within settings involving violent graphic imagery and a violent
551	narrative backdrop.
552	Participants were assigned to play a graphically violent game
553	after completing a self-report assessment of trait aggression and
554	before assessments of needs and motivation. Results showed that,
555	as predicted within CET, autonomy and competence need satis-
556	faction derived from play accounted for increased levels of game
557	enjoyment, immersion, and preference for future play. Further-
558	more, individual differences in aggression were independent pre-
559	dictors of preference for future play, although interestingly not of
560	enjoyment during game play. These results indicated that need
561	satisfaction was a consistent motivator of play across all partici-
562	pants, but that those high in aggression exhibited a greater desire
563	to play the violent game in the future despite not enjoying it, and
564	that despite low enjoyment, they felt more immersed compared
565	with participants lower in trait aggression.
566	In a third study, we wanted to establish a causal direction for the
567	effects we observed between level of game violence and player
568	preference. To do this, we implemented a design used in a widely
569	cited study that focused on the effects of high- versus low-violence
570	video game play (Anderson et al., 2004) in which players were
571	randomly assigned to play either a high-violence or nonviolent
572	game. We added an assessment of trait-level aggression before
573	play and after-play assessments of motivational constructs. We
574	expected that need satisfaction would account for game enjoyment,
575	immersion, and preference for future play between participants and
576	within games independent of their content, and that trait-level
577	aggression would moderate the relation between the level of vio-
578	lence present in game content and preference for play such that
579	those high in aggression would be more likely want opportunities
580	for future high-violence play than low-aggression persons. Results
581	showed that the games did not vary in the enjoyment or need
582	satisfaction they provided, and that violent content by itself was
583	not signiﬁcantly related to enjoyment. Nonetheless, those high in
584	trait aggression did report more preference for the violent game.
585	In the previous study, we used two different games that had
586	violent versus nonviolent content. This design was concerning
587	because the different games varied across a number of potentially
588	confounding dimensions, including control interface complexity,
589	graphic elements, game action, and many other dimensions besides
590	violent content. The fourth study provided a more rigorous test of
591	the motivating role of game violence. To this end, we extensively
592	modiﬁed the content but not play mechanics of Half-Life 2 ,a
593	popular computer game. The high-violence version provided
594	graphic violence featuring ﬁrearms, gore, and a conﬂict-based
595	kill-or-be-killed narrative, whereas the low-violence version had a
596	friendly competition story describing the play as a game of tag
597	wherein players teleported adversaries away. Following training in
598	the game controls, we randomly assigned participants to high-
599	violence or low-violence versions of the game. Results showed
600	that the violent and nonviolent versions did not differ in the need
601	satisfaction, enjoyment, or immersion they inspired. Of interest
602	was the ﬁnding that in-game competence and autonomy need
603	satisfaction, not level of violence, accounted for variability in these
604	outcomes. Furthermore, trait aggression related to preference for
605	future play only in the high-violence condition, thereby moderat-
606	ing the link between level of game violence and preference for
607	future play. The core motivation for all participants was rooted in
608	need satisfaction provided by the challenges and opportunities
609	present in both conditions, but those with high (relative to low)
610	levels of aggression expressed greater desire to play the violent
611	variation of the game in the future.
612	Two additional studies in this series extended the generalizabil-
613	ity of our ﬁndings to experienced players. Our ﬁfth study was an
614	experimental design that randomly assigned experienced young
615	male gamers to play a relatively violent game with either realistic
616	or abstracted gore. Even among this cohort, analyses revealed that
617	more graphic violence increased game appeal only for those rela-
618	tively high in aggression. The violent version of the game was
619	preferred only by those relatively high in trait aggression. Our
620	sixth study surveyed players about their current favorite video
621	game, and showed that play that supported the basic needs for
622	competence and autonomy produced more immersive and enjoy-
623	able experiences relating to greater preference for future play.
624	Level of game violence did not account for the appeal of games; in
625	fact, it was negatively related to game enjoyment once need
626	satisfaction was accounted for. Violence did, however, relate to
627	valuing and preference for future play as a function of the level of
628	player aggression. For those low in trait-level aggression, level of
629	game violence served to undermine these outcomes.
630	We derived a number of novel ﬁndings by investigating violent
631	game content in terms of its motivational value rather than its
632	potential to incite aggression. First, we found that violent content
633	does not by itself contribute to the appeal of games, a result that
634	runs counter to the commonly held view that popular violent
635	games such as Grand Theft Auto IV are popular because they are
636	violent. Instead, we found that most popular violent games moti-
637	vate engagement for players of subclinical levels of aggression as
638	a function of the competence and autonomy need satisfaction they
639	provide. Second, we found that violent game content did appeal to
640	a subset of players: those relatively high in aggression. This
641	ﬁnding suggests that a subpopulation of individuals might be more
642	liable to seek out violent content across a broad range of media
643	(e.g., ﬁlm, music, literature) and could be more vulnerable to its
644	inﬂuence. Finally, we found that violent game content can serve to
645	undermine the appeal of games for those with moderate to low
646	levels of aggression. This result indicates that video game creators
647	could potentially be alienating a broad audience when they infuse
648	inherently motivating game mechanics with gratuitous violence.
649	Sources of Player Aggression
650	The potential of video games to inﬂuence human aggression is
651	a highly controversial subject, both in popular culture and increas-
652	ingly in scientiﬁc circles. The empirical study of player aggression
653	focuses on how the violent content and themes of games can serve
654	as a source of increased aggression postplay, especially among
655	highly aggressive individuals (Giumetti & Markey, 2007). Given
656	the predominance of interest in violent game content, it is note-
657	worthy that little research to date has examined how game struc-
658	ture might also shape short-term shifts in aggression through
659	motivational processes. Past SDT research has shown that moti-
660	vational processes based on psychological need deprivation can
661	lead to more aggressive behavior in real-world contexts (Assor,
662	Roth, & Deci, 2004; Ryan & Grolnick, 1986). Related research
663	indicates that interfering with performance in goal pursuit, even in
664	trivial tasks, can elicit heightened aggression (Berkowitz, 1989;
665	Pedersen, Gonzales, & Miller, 2000). Based on our motivational
666	159SPECIAL ISSUE: A MOTIVATIONAL MODEL OF VIDEO GAME ENGAGEMENT
667	
668	approach, we expected that video game play could serve to in-
669	crease player aggression independent of game content (e.g., vio-
670	lence) when experiences of play undermine the basic psycholog-
671	ical needs of players. As a speciﬁc example, we expected that
672	mastery of controls (i.e., the player’s ability to enact actions in the
673	game world through a controller) could be linked to aggression
674	when it frustrates feelings of competence. That is, overly complex
675	or nonintuitive control interfaces are likely to frustrate the com-
676	petence needs and lead to enhanced postplay levels of aggression.
677	To test this, Przybylski (2009) looked at competence frustration
678	and player aggression in a recent series of studies.
679	The ﬁrst study replicated the basic design of a violent video
680	game study conducted by Anderson and colleagues (2004). The
681	main purpose of drawing on this method was to examine how
682	experiences that supported versus undermined mastery of controls
683	inﬂuenced short-term shifts in player aggression. It was important
684	to isolate this effect from differences in the level of violent game
685	content. The nonviolent game used in the original study of Ander-
686	son et al. (2004) had a relatively simple control interface, whereas
687	the violent game had a much more difﬁcult and complex one. It
688	was expected that participants randomly assigned to play the
689	nonviolent game would therefore experience greater mastery of
690	controls than those playing the violent game because only four
691	keyboard keys were required to play the former, whereas a mouse
692	and more than a dozen keys were needed in the latter. Furthermore,
693	it was hypothesized that an experience of need deprivation, re-
694	ﬂected by low scores on the self-report of mastery of controls,
695	would lead to pre- to postplay increases in state-level aggression.
696	Also, it was expected that such shifts in aggression would be
697	negatively related to game enjoyment. Results largely supported
698	these intuitions. Players assigned to play the violent game that
699	featured a more complex control interface experienced lower mas-
700	tery of controls compared with those who played the nonviolent
701	game. In addition, pre- to postplay increases in aggression were in
702	evidence for participants assigned to play both games when they
703	experienced low mastery of controls. Increased levels of aggres-
704	sion in turn related to lower levels of game enjoyment. In sum-
705	mary, it was an inability to master the game controls instead of
706	differences in violent game content that contributed to the ob-
707	served shifts from pre- to postplay aggression.
708	A second study examined how the inherent complexity of video
709	game control schemes could increase player aggression entirely
710	independent of contributions of violent game content. Two exper-
711	iments, one focusing on a violent game, the other on a nonviolent
712	game, manipulated the complexity of the control interfaces used to
713	interact with game challenges. In both experiments, it was pre-
714	dicted that mastery of controls would be the lowest and pre- to
715	postplay shifts in aggression would be the greatest for participants
716	randomly assigned to games using more complex interfaces. Also,
717	it was expected that increases in aggression for players of each
718	type of game would lead to less game enjoyment. Results showed
719	that complex interfaces undermined player competence and fos-
720	tered increases in aggression in both experiments. Increased ag-
721	gression related negatively to game enjoyment for participants
722	assigned to play high- and low-violence games.
723	A third study tested how opportunities to practice with a com-
724	plex game’s control interface before playing shaped how the game
725	inﬂuenced short-term shifts in player aggression. Przybylski
726	(2009) identiﬁed practice as an important factor to consider in light
727	of a meta-analysis of video game play and aggression reported by
728	Sherry (2001). This study demonstrated that the length of game
729	engagement moderated the links observed between type of game
730	and aggression; that is, aggression was lowest for players of
731	high-violence games when they were assigned to play these games
732	for long periods of time. When viewed through the motivational
733	lens of our model, this result suggests that longer play periods
734	provide opportunities for participants to develop mastery over
735	game controls. In the ﬁrst experiment, participants were provided
736	the opportunity to learn the controls and mechanics of a game with
737	complex controls before being assigned to face challenges that
738	featured high- or low-violence stories and acts. The second exper-
739	iment mirrored the ﬁrst in most respects but provided a practice
740	period (to teach game controls and mechanics) to only half of the
741	participants. Level of violent content related to increased feelings
742	of threat in participants assigned to high-violence play in both
743	experiments, but content was on balance unrelated to shifts in
744	aggression. Holding violence content constant, results from the
745	ﬁrst experiment demonstrated that players who felt competent at
746	the game controls felt less aggressive following play. Additional
747	ﬁndings from the second experiment showed that participants who
748	were provided practice time developed greater mastery of controls,
749	whereas those who had not practiced incurred increased aggression
750	pre- to postplay.
751	A fourth study aimed to test the generalizability of need support
752	versus deprivation as a source of aggression on the between- and
753	within-persons levels in a sample of avid video game players. It
754	was predicted that experiences of play characterized by compe-
755	tence need satisfaction would relate to lower postplay aggression,
756	higher game enjoyment, higher postplay positive affect, and lower
757	postplay negative affect. A sample of avid players provided data
758	on three games they were currently playing, their experiences of
759	need satisfaction, and postplay affect. These games were coded for
760	level of violence using the methods we had used when studying the
761	appeal of violence in games. Multilevel modeling (Bryk & Rau-
762	denbush, 1992) was used to isolate the effects between games.
763	Results showed that game engagement that supported the basic
764	psychological needs predicted greater game enjoyment and post-
765	play positive affect as well as lower levels of postplay aggression
766	and negative affect. On the other hand, consistent with previous
767	studies, violent content did not signiﬁcantly relate to player out-
768	comes when in-game need satisfaction was considered.
769	In sum, these recent studies suggest that the extent to which the
770	features of play support versus undermine basic psychological
771	needs ought to be considered as a robust source of variability in
772	postgame player aggression and negative affect. The results
773	showed that although the level of violence in game content did not
774	consistently account for short-term shifts in aggression, structural
775	elements of games such as interface complexity and the provision
776	of practice carried robust motivational and well-being implications
777	for players.
778	This pattern of results suggests that future research focused on
779	violent game content and short-term shifts in aggression should be
780	wary of using different games that vary across motivational di-
781	mensions to represent high- versus low-violence play experiences.
782	It also further testiﬁes to the utility of the needs model in locating
783	some of the concern-focused outcomes in video game research.
784	The needs model provides a new, motivation-based way of study-
785	ing player aggression among those with subclinical levels of trait
786	160 PRZYBYLSKI, RIGBY, AND RYAN
787	
788	aggression, an approach markedly different from the dominant
789	social-learning viewpoint.
790	Having Versus Wanting to Play
791	Many commentators have expressed concern about the overuse
792	of video games and their potential to undermine healthy develop-
793	ment. Supporting such concerns are research ﬁndings suggesting
794	that the quantity of time players devote to games is negatively
795	correlated with player well-being and adjustment (Gru ¨sser et al.,
796	2007). Beyond this correlational link, however, little is understood
797	about the circumstances that make some individuals vulnerable to
798	the overuse and disordered use of games or the factors that make
799	others resilient. A recent study by Przybylski, Weinstein, Ryan,
800	and Rigby (2009) used SDT as a conceptual and empirical bridge
801	to examine patterns of obsessive play and overuse. The aim was to
802	explore whether individual differences in basic psychological need
803	satisfaction in everyday life could serve as a protective factor
804	against unhealthy forms of play and need deprivation could serve
805	as a risk factor. Even more speciﬁcally, we wondered whether
806	players might even derive different beneﬁts from video game
807	engagement as a function of their styles of play.
808	The SDT perspective holds that individuals who have their basic
809	needs satisﬁed in daily life are more likely to experience volition
810	and choice and have high levels of global well-being compared
811	with those who have their needs undermined (Ryan & Deci,
812	2000b). Accordingly, Przybylski, Weinstein, et al. (2009) pre-
813	dicted that individuals who have their needs for competence,
814	autonomy, and relatedness satisﬁed in daily life would experience
815	more sense of choice about their engagement in video games and
816	would be more likely to play because they want to. In contrast,
817	SDT research has shown that the undermining of basic needs
818	exposes individuals to less self-determined, more compulsive be-
819	haviors (Deci & Ryan, 2000). In line with this, Przybylski, Wein-
820	stein, et al. expected that individuals with low levels of need
821	satisfaction would feel more compelled to engage games or to
822	report that they often play because they “have to.”
823	To examine these questions, we applied the dualistic model of
824	passion (Vallerand, 2008), a motivational theory in line with SDT.
825	The dualistic model of passion has previously been applied to
826	Internet use (Seguin-Levesque, Laliberte, Pelletier, Blanchard, &
827	Vallerand, 2003) and video gaming domains (Wang, Khoo, Liu, &
828	Divaharan, 2008), and thus provides a means for assessing “want-
829	ing to” versus “having to” play. Results from these previous
830	studies have shown that players often engage in computer activi-
831	ties through a harmonious passion: They have a strong inclination
832	to engage that feels consistent with abiding values and motiva-
833	tions. Previous results also have suggested that people with har-
834	monious passion experience more positive outcomes from com-
835	puter activities (e.g., self-determined relationships, positive affect).
836	Conversely, some people have an obsessive passion for computer
837	activities. Like harmonious participants, they have an equally
838	powerful motivation to play, but it is experienced as a more
839	compulsive and uncontrollable urge to act, and is predictive of
840	more negative outcomes associated with play. Our interest was in
841	predicting harmonious and obsessive engagement in video games
842	as a function of need satisfaction (or its deprivation) in everyday
843	life.
844	Przybylski, Weinstein, et al. (2009) sampled a large number of
845	regular video game players and assessed individual differences in
846	basic psychological need satisfaction, motives for play, mood
847	immediately after play, and overall levels of player well-being. It
848	was expected that individuals high in basic need satisfaction would
849	pursue play with a sense of volition, as a harmonious passion, and
850	that those unable to access need satisfactions in day-to-day life
851	would be more likely to engage games with a sense of compulsion,
852	as an obsessive passion. Przybylski, Weinstein, et al. predicted that
853	harmonious passion for play would relate to greater game enjoy-
854	ment and postplay mood, and obsessive passion would be linked
855	with a greater quantity of play and poorer mood following play.
856	Results showed that individual differences in basic need satis-
857	faction were associated with the way in which individuals engaged
858	video games. Across a diverse variety of favored games, those who
859	experienced high levels of need satisfaction had more harmonious
860	passion for play, which in turn was linked to an enhancing pattern:
861	more game enjoyment and positive postplay mood. In contrast,
862	low levels of need satisfaction predicted obsessive passion for
863	play, which in turn was associated with a disordered pattern: less
864	game enjoyment, negative postplay mood, and yet a higher overall
865	amount of engagement.
866	Two additional ﬁndings of interest were uncovered. First, when
867	individual differences in need satisfaction were accounted for,
868	passion for video game play related robustly to player well-being
869	tied to the gaming context (mood immediately after play), but it
870	was not uniquely related to overall (global) levels of player well-
871	being. That is, although harmonious versus obsessive game play
872	had associations with general well-being, it was no longer predic-
873	tive when accounting for everyday need satisfaction. This suggests
874	that video game styles may be more a symptom than a cause of
875	psychological distress. In other words, a disordered pattern of
876	game engagement can be thought of as one consequence (among
877	many) of psychological need deprivation in day-to-day life. Sec-
878	ond, high numbers of hours of video game play were not invari-
879	antly related to negative outcomes. When they were, it was in part
880	because players were obsessively engaged. Analyses showed that
881	obsessive passion moderated the link between the amount of game
882	engagement and player outcomes, such that a high level of hours
883	led to negative outcomes insofar as they were accompanied by a
884	compulsive drive to play. Taken together, these ﬁndings suggest
885	that future video game research focused on disordered play should
886	account for the quantity and motivational quality of game engage-
887	ment. The direct inﬂuence of video game play on overall well-
888	being, for good or ill, may be much less signiﬁcant than previously
889	thought.
890	The Moderating Role of Immersion
891	Video games can provide highly immersive experiences, allow-
892	ing the player to experience a strong sense of presence in the game
893	world. Immersion reﬂects an illusion of nonmediation between the
894	player and the gaming context, so that the player feels directly
895	embedded in the virtual environment and the story happening there
896	(Lombard & Ditton, 1997; Rigby, 2004). The ability of video
897	games to immerse participants might shape how the content of
898	video games serves to inﬂuence player psychology. Our research
899	approach trifurcates the general immersion state into three sub-
900	components or subscales: physical presence , feeling as if one is
901	161SPECIAL ISSUE: A MOTIVATIONAL MODEL OF VIDEO GAME ENGAGEMENT
902	
903	actually in the world; emotional presence, feeling that game events
904	have real emotional weight; and narrative presence , having a
905	personal investment and engagement with the story (Ryan et al.,
906	2006). Although these three components of immersion are thought
907	to be highly collinear, they have different proﬁles within different
908	games; for example, some games have little narrative but afford a
909	great physical sense of presence.
910	Many game designers and players assume that a sense of im-
911	mersion is based mainly on the ﬁdelity of the graphics and sound
912	provided by a virtual environment. It is interesting that our ﬁndings
913	suggest a minor role for these elements. Instead, the major predic-
914	tor of presence is the degree to which games satisfy motivational
915	needs. Video game play that satisﬁes the needs for competence,
916	autonomy, and relatedness robustly increases a player’s sense of
917	immersion, both across different game types (Ryan et al., 2006)
918	and game contents (Przybylski et al., 2009). In other words, when
919	players have their needs satisﬁed within the game, they are more
920	phenomenologically embedded in the emotional, physical, and
921	narrative elements of the game world.
922	Although the immersive dimensions of video games have been
923	points of interest in writing by researchers studying aggression
924	(Carnagey & Anderson, 2004) and disordered play (Gru ¨sser et al.,
925	2007), the moderating inﬂuence of immersion in virtual contexts
926	has not been examined. Recent research by Weinstein, Przybylski,
927	and Ryan (2009) was the ﬁrst to empirically evaluate how immer-
928	sion shapes links between the content of virtual contexts and
929	real-world interpersonal goals and decision making.
930	In the ﬁrst study, participants engaged one of two virtual con-
931	texts: One featured manufactured imagery including cities and
932	skyscrapers; the other had natural settings such as forests and
933	streams. Based on past research (Kasser, 2002; Schultz, 2002), it
934	was hypothesized that participants exposed to the manufactured
935	imagery would espouse increased self-focused or self-centered
936	goals, whereas those shown natural imagery would have higher
937	levels of other-focused or prosocial goals. More important for this
938	discussion, Weinstein et al. (2009) predicted that immersion, as-
939	sessed using an adapted version of the Physical Presence subscale
940	of the Player Experience of Need Satisfaction Scale, would am-
941	plify the effects of content (manufactured vs. natural) on shifts in
942	interpersonal goals. Results supported these expectations: Manu-
943	factured content led to greater self-focused goals, and natural
944	content was linked to greater prosocial goals. Of key interest,
945	however, was the ﬁnding that these shifts in interpersonal goals
946	were signiﬁcantly more pronounced for participants who were
947	highly immersed in the virtual contexts.
948	Expanding on these ﬁndings, two additional experiments pre-
949	sented by Weinstein et al. (2009) explored mechanisms through
950	which immersion ampliﬁes this carryover effect. Results from a
951	second study showed that immersion enhanced the link between
952	nature content and prosocial goals because high levels of immer-
953	sion ampliﬁed the extent to which participants felt connected to
954	nature as a whole. Similarly, participants who were highly im-
955	mersed in manufactured content had more self-focused goals in-
956	sofar as immersion in this content undermined basic needs. That is,
957	experiencing oneself as physically present in a virtual environment
958	was shown to moderate the main effect of content per se. The third
959	study replicated and extended these results, and showed that im-
960	mersion also played a role in shaping actual decision-making
961	behavior.
962	Although not focused directly on concerns about aggression or
963	behavioral dependence, the work of Weinstein and colleagues
964	(2009) suggests that feeling physically present in a virtual context
965	can intensify the carryover of the content of that context into
966	real-world cognition and behavior. Weinstein et al.’s approach and
967	results have special signiﬁcance for future video game research
968	when set against those of Ryan et al. (2006) and Przybylski et al.
969	(2009), which showed that the physical, emotional, and narrative
970	facets of presence are based on the basic need satisfaction games
971	provide. Given that these elements of immersion could increase or
972	decrease the carryover effects of game content into real-world
973	outcomes as a function of need satisfaction, future video game
974	research concerned with disordered play or player aggression
975	should consider immersion as a key moderating construct and
976	assess or manipulate it in the course of inquiry.
977	Other Perspectives on Video Game Motivation
978	As noted above, most social and personality psychology re-
979	search begins examining the inﬂuences of video games by quan-
980	tifying the extent to which engagement is linked with negative
981	psychosocial outcomes (e.g., aggression, behavioral dependence).
982	Research based on the mass communications theory focuses more
983	attention on factors that inﬂuence player motivation. We very
984	brieﬂy highlight these approaches and compare them with our
985	motivational perspective.
986	Uses and gratiﬁcations theory (Blumler & Katz, 1974), a general
987	framework intended to describe how different media (e.g., televised
988	sports) serve as solutions to everyday problems (e.g., boredom), is the
989	main model applied to games in the communications domain.Sherry
990	and Lucas (2003) applied uses and gratiﬁcations theory to create a
991	statistically validated taxonomy of the reasons individuals hold for
992	engaging in video games. Their ﬁndings suggest that players use
993	games as a means of accessing one (or more) of the following
994	psychological states: (a) competition, experience defeating others;
995	(b) challenge, experience success following effort; (c) diversion,
996	escape an experience of stress; (d) fantasy, experience novel or
997	unrealistic stimuli; (d) social interaction, have a social experience;
998	and (f) arousal, experience activated positive emotions such as
999	excitement. In addition, they found that these reasons were more or
1000	less prevalent with respect to some kinds of games and were linked
1001	to different levels of engagement. For example, video game en-
1002	gagement enacted to socialize with friends or escape aversive
1003	psychological states related to more hours of play each week.
1004	Further research by Lucas and Sherry (2004) extended the appli-
1005	cation of uses and gratiﬁcations theory, showing that gender-based
1006	differences between preferences for some kinds of video games
1007	(e.g., HALO vs. The Sims ) could be explained, in part, by differ-
1008	ences between the satisfaction players of each gender anticipated
1009	deriving from engagement. For example, Lucas and Sherry (2004)
1010	showed that females preferred games that conveyed the experience
1011	of successful completion of challenges compared with those that
1012	imparted a sense of domination of others.
1013	Although they might seem topically similar, the uses and grat-
1014	iﬁcations theory approach differs in many ways from the motiva-
1015	tional model we are presenting. The SDT- and CET-based ap-
1016	proach views sustained video game engagement as a function of a
1017	set of basic psychological needs that video game play may or may
1018	not fulﬁll. In our view, competence, autonomy, and relatedness are
1019	162 PRZYBYLSKI, RIGBY, AND RYAN
1020	
1021	irreducible, universal human needs. Studies conducted in a wide
1022	range of cultures have shown that basic need support is linked to
1023	intrinsic motivation (e.g., Chirkov & Ryan, 2001; Deci et al., 1999;
1024	Jang, Reeve, Ryan, & Kim, 2009). This consistency adds utility to
1025	our model for understanding the inﬂuence of games; if it is known
1026	to satisfy basic needs, there is reason to expect it will have positive
1027	inﬂuences in other samples. The uses and gratiﬁcations theory
1028	focuses instead on differences in what satisfaction people con-
1029	sciously seek in media use, and research demonstrates that such
1030	satisfaction varies with media type (Greenberg & Hnilo, 1996), a
1031	media user’s gender (Lucas & Sherry, 2004), or culture (Green-
1032	berg, Li, Ku, & Tokinoya, 1991). Yet, SDT has a long tradition of
1033	showing that people’s goals, even when fulﬁlled, do not always
1034	yield need satisfaction, and therefore do not always predict per-
1035	sistence or well-being outcomes (Ryan, Kasser, Sheldon, & Deci,
1036	1996).
1037	Based on theoretical work of Bartle (2004), recent research
1038	conducted by Yee (2006a, 2006b) focuses on the motives that
1039	different types of players adopt when engaging popular online
1040	games (e.g., World of Warcraft ). This approach links the subjec-
1041	tive importance players place on actions they enact in the game
1042	world to differences in player demographics and the amount of
1043	time individuals spend playing. Yee (2006a) identiﬁed three over-
1044	arching motives for in-game behavior: (1) achievement, accumu-
1045	lation of status or power; (2) socializing, formation and mainte-
1046	nance of relationships; and (3) immersion, exploration of the
1047	game’s narrative, locales, and characters. Yee (2006b) reported
1048	that female players placed greater emphasis on the socializing and
1049	immersion motives, whereas male players considered achievement
1050	motives most important. Furthermore, Yee’s research showed that

verdicts.claim: The appeal and well-being effects of video games derive from their capacity to satisfy the three basic psychological needs of self-determination theo
verdicts.note: Paper states verbatim that appeal and well-being effects are based in satisfying needs for competence, autonomy, and relatedness, and that 'the SDT model of need satisfaction predicts sustained engagement over time, as well as the short-term effects of game activities on players' well-being.'

verdicts.claim: In Ryan, Rigby & Przybylski's (2006) study of online game players, competence, autonomy, and relatedness need satisfaction each made independent cont
verdicts.note: The paper reports of the final Ryan et al. (2006) study (self-selecting players): 'all three needs made independent contributions to game enjoyment, immersion, and estimations of future engagement,' with only autonomy and competence accounting for unique incremental variance in postplay affect.

verdicts.claim: Intuitive mastery of a game's controls/interface is a necessary-but-not-sufficient gate for engagement: players must feel intuitive command of basic
verdicts.note: Text states mastery of controls 'constituted a necessary but not sufficient condition' (results supported this), and Przybylski (2009) studies show complex interfaces and lack of practice time frustrated competence and produced pre- to postplay aggression increases — 'it was an inability to master the game controls instead of differences in violent game content' that drove aggression shifts.

verdicts.claim: Violent content is not, on average, a significant motivator of play once need satisfaction is accounted for — autonomy and competence satisfaction, n
verdicts.note: Paper states verbatim 'violent game content is not, on average, a significant motivator of play,' that autonomy/competence satisfaction accounted for enjoyment, immersion, and preference for future play, and that these processes are 'robust predictors over and above differences in player demographics' applying 'across game genres and content.'

verdicts.claim: Players whose basic psychological needs are unmet in everyday life develop an 'obsessive passion' for games — a compulsive engagement pattern marked
verdicts.note: Paper reports low everyday need satisfaction predicted obsessive passion linked to 'a disordered pattern: less game enjoyment, negative postplay mood, and yet a higher overall amount of engagement,' while need-satisfied players showed harmonious, volitional engagement; the 'dark-pattern-exploitable' phrasing is the claimant's framing, not attributed to the paper.
